data.title: As The South Korean Market Crumbles, Coupang Is Worth Watching
data.description:
I wrote about KT earlier this week. That’s not a plug, but rather it got me thinking a lot about South Korea and the flight to safety toward telecoms on the major stock market drop we’ve seen there, and indeed we’ve seen most everywhere. Today I thought I’d stick with South Korea, but take a look at a once popular growth stock there, Coupang (NYSE:CPNG). They’ve had hard times lately, but that’s no surprise.

Coupang has been growing like gangbusters for years, and we’ll be looking at the growth under the lens of the current, relatively cheap entry point. We’ll be looking at their success this year (on a non-GAAP basis) and also reconciling the very confusing GAAP bottom line this year, which was related both to tax differences and how South Korea handles a fine levied that’s still under appeal.

We’ll be having a look at their avenues for growth, and also the potential risk that surrounds them based on the reality that they’re an American corporation operating in South Korea during the global tariff crisis.

Understanding Coupang

If we were in South Korea, you’d all immediately know who we’re talking about here. It might not be immediately clear for investors elsewhere, however, so as a brief primer on the matter, Coupang envisions itself as becoming the “Amazon of South Korea.”

Their business has many of the things you’d be familiar with from that goal, from a major shopping network, their own delivery system, even a premium service branded WOW which functions similarly to Amazon Prime, faster free delivery and video content and such. Beyond the obvious shopping service, they do Coupang Eats, which is a sort of DoorDash type service, a grocery delivery service like Instacart, a payment system, etc. Pretty much every e-commerce thing you can think of, Coupang is trying to do in South Korea in one form or another.

Beyond that, Coupang is looking to expand into other countries, with Coupang Taiwan on small but growing effort, which we will discuss later. In January 2024, they also acquired Farfetch Holdings, a marketplace service for the luxury fashion industry. That too we will consider later. First though let’s break down the finances.

2024 Broadly Successful, Despite Complexities of Numbers

2023

2024

Net Revenue

$24.4 billion

$30.3 billion

Operating Income

$473 million

$436 million

Income Tax Expense

($776 million)

$407 million

Non-GAAP EBITDA Margin

4.4%

4.5%

Net Income

$1.36 billion

$154 million

GAAP diluted EPS

75¢

8¢

Non-GAAP EPS

26¢

22¢

(source: most recent 10-K from SEC)

Here we can see some of the complexities of Coupang’s numbers in 2024. You’re not reading it wrong, they booked a $776 million gain on income taxes in 2023 and an expense of $407 million in 2024. That’s because of an unusual change of valuation allowances amounting to around $1 billion. In this case, we can see where the non-GAAP version, which doesn’t include such one-off issues, might be more indicative of where Coupang is really going.

The difference in GAAP and non-GAAP in 2024 is centered primarily around $127 million in acquisition-related costs for the Farfetch acquisition and a $121 million fine by the KFTC for alleged violations of the Monopoly Regulation and Fair Trade Act. That fine is still subject to an appeal, but South Korean law requires them to start paying even as the appeals process continues, and the fine was booked in 2024, even though the payment plan is covering multiple years, and the company is holding out hope it will be reversed in court entirely.

The big takeaways here are that revenue continued to grow strongly, and they resoundingly beat non-GAAP EPS estimates. The adjusted EBITDA margin remains razor-thin, but it’s improved slightly, so that’s another positive. The non-GAAP P/E is still 89 at the time I’m writing this, so it’s still substantially higher than the sector median, so if this company is to be justified as an investment, it’s going to have to be on the basis of expected growth.

Taiwan and Robots: The Avenues of Earnings Growth

Coupang has two obvious avenues for future improvement in bottom line numbers. Those are expanding the revenue and improving efficiencies. In both of those situations the opportunities are substantial.

Coupang has seen strong growth of its Coupang Taiwan subsidiary, and is also moving toward a restaurant delivery service in Japan, after a previous attempt to establish themselves in Japan was unsuccessful. Taiwan revenue alone grew 23% quarter over quarter in Q4. The biggest growth opportunity however, is probably within South Korea itself.

Though Coupang is nearly a household name there, and styles itself the Amazon of South Korea, it is by no means as dominant as Amazon is the United States. There is plenty of room to improve the business and grow revenue within their primary market, and beyond that, improving efficiency to increase margins is another strategy to make the earnings rise.

And that’s where the robots come in. Coupang is building its business around the idea of efficiency and automation making things ever more convenient for the customers. Those efforts and the relative size of South Korea make it possible to offer same-day delivery, or in the case of night orders, even delivery by dawn to customers. Place the order just before midnight and get it delivered by 7:00 AM, that’s where they’re at in some cases already, and improvements will allow even more products to get delivered that efficiently.

Coupang emphasized their intention to drive margin growth through automation and robots during their most recent conference call, and noted the percent of their infrastructure upgraded to where they want it is only in the low teens right now, so there is ample room for further growth.

Estimates are that in 2025 we’ll see revenue grow to $34.34 billion, and EPS increase to 36¢. In 2026, both get better still, $39.58 billion in revenue and 73¢ in EPS. If things go right, revenue and margin growth could make that happen, and possibly even more. Though this translates to forward P/Es that would still be too high for a value investment, the undeniable momentum in their growth positions them nicely as a growth stock.

Farfetch and Its Prospects

As mentioned above, Coupang acquired Farfetch in January 2024. Farfetch was a world-wide marketplace company that delivers luxury fashion products for various partnered brands and boutiques. Farfetch had quite a substantial customer base, but it was losing hundreds of millions of dollars annually, which is why Coupang was able to buy it for a fairly modest price.

Luckily, Coupang is very experienced at running large marketplaces, and their focus on improving efficiency allowed them to quickly pare away the losses. In the Q4 conference call, they reported reaching effective break-even on Farfetch in just a single year, and with a fairly minimal loss of scale. It’s looking like it could well be a good purchase.

Honestly, Farfetch is probably unlikely to ever be a massive part of Coupang’s portfolio, but if they prove successful in turning it from a big, losing operation into a profitable one, Coupang could well use it as a model for other acquisitions in the future. It’s early to say, but things are looking very promising in that regard.

An American E-Commerce Company In the Age of Tariff Wars

Beyond the risks that a major e-commerce company always faces, Coupang has another substantial risk I would be remiss if I didn’t bring up, the tariff war situation. As I write this, I see that there has been a pause on most of the tariffs, and the market predictably rebounded.

Still, it’s not a resolution, it’s just a pause, and if nothing else changes, we could easily be in the exact same situation 90 days hence. That’s a potential problem for everyone, but potentially a bigger problem for a company like Coupang, which is incorporated in the US, but conducts its operations almost exclusively overseas.

Call it what you like, but this tariff war is leading to a rise in panic and anti-American sentiment across the world. Since the starts and stops of this on-again, off-again crisis are being driven more or less exclusively by the US government, all things American are being viewed in a different light.

Historically, South Korea was very favorable toward the US, because of their role in defense in the Korean War (which 72 years later still hasn’t officially ended because a peace treaty was never signed). The untold billions of dollars this trade war could cost the South Korean economy is a straightforward way that historic goodwill could evaporate, and Coupang’s status as an American company could suddenly become a liability that it wasn’t before.

A Notable Entry Level, But for the Situation

Where does that leave us? It’s complicated.

Coupang has the look of an excellent growth company, and trading on the lower end of their 52-week range, I feel like in normal circumstances I’d rate it a buy. That said, it’s not normal circumstances, and I’m simply going to rate it a hold for now.

Coupang has excellent potential, but the risk of suffering during a global recession caused by whatever comes of this crisis is too great, and we might find an even lower entry point than where we’re at right now, that would make it even more enticing. For now, it’s a watch list stock for sure, but I wouldn’t go any further until we get some clarity on where global trade, and the economy, is headed.

Investors who do jump in right now should keep a close eye on where their adjusted EBITDA margin heads in the next year, and whether they continue to meet expectations with their revenue growth. Either one of those turning away from the direction they’ve been headed is likely the first indication we’ll get that the crisis is doing them substantial harm. In that case, a lower entry point would definitely be better.

data.title: Trump Tariffs Raise Questions In Midstream
data.description:
The Trump Administration has a problem. On the one hand, President Trump has emphasized the importance of "energy dominance"; the maximization of fossil fuels production and the export of American energy globally to exert influence and support allies. On the other hand, he has also criticized domestic gasoline and electricity prices, and has pressured both OPEC and domestic producers to keep prices low.

Exporting energy, all else equal, tightens domestic supply and contributes to higher local prices. Encouraging exports, while it does support job creation and trade balance, works against keeping energy costs down at home. And that's a primary reason why he was elected in the first place. Reconciling those goals is tricky, and we're starting to see the impact of that. Today, I'm going to talk about how that plays into the top companies in the midstream space, and cornerstone midstream firms: Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), Targa Resources (TRGP), and Energy Transfer (ET) immediately spring to mind.

Tariff Escalation

The current tariff now sits at 125% on United States exports by China, and quite frankly, given the way this has been going, it could be higher or gone completely by the time I press publish on this research. Originally, Chinese tariffs targeted liquefied natural gas ("LNG") and crude oil. Other energy products like natural gas liquids ("NGLs") were excluded. That now is no longer the case. Tariffs are not the only way these trade flows are impacted. With the United States also considering port fees for Chinese built ships - which is basically a humongous chunk of the seaborne market - costs also would increase just to get the product out of America.

This is a big escalation for China. They import very little crude oil from the United States, and while they are a buyer of American LNG, they have other buying options. NGLs are a different story, specifically propane and ethane, are a different story. Propane is actually one of the highest valued imports into China, and a quarter of all propane that leaves the United States is destined for Chinese ports for use in the production of propylene and cooking / heating. For ethane, Chinese petrochemical crackers that use ethane as a feedstock to create ethylene rely entirely on American exports. These Chinese facilities simply cannot absorb the cost and remain economical versus ethylene produced in other ways. They will likely have to shut down, but given half of waterborne ethane out of the United States ends up in China, it will also significantly disrupt the ethane export industry.

China did not do this without thinking. While it employed similar retaliatory tariffs in 2018 during the last bout of trade tensions, it is much more reliant today. But even back then, Chinese buyers did not absorb the cost - they just scaled back purchases. Rerouting global trade this time around is impossible, but I suspect austerity from China on this issue.

No question, this is a big deal for these three midstream shops. Most know that both companies have substantially committed to the NGL production chain. They dominate the ownership of gas processing plants, NGL pipelines, fractionation at Mont Belvieu, and the ownership of NGL export port infrastructure.

Percent Of Proceed Contracts

Unlike crude oil and natural gas, midstream gas processors most frequently use percent of proceeds ("POP") contracts. When they break down the raw natural gas liquids into their varying separate products, they receive a fee based on the final wholesale value. This makes them price sensitive; if propane and ethane values fall, the money they make falls.

These contracts typically do have fee floors; companies were bumping up against them in late 2023 / early 2024, and that was at prices not all that far away from where we were today. But it's still an unfortunate situation, as higher NGL prices were a big driver of predicted EBITDA improvement for these companies in 2025.

If exports are cut, the question also becomes what happens with the excess product. Even at much lower prices, domestic consumption is not going to rise much, and NGL production was also set to rise materially this year. All of this was coming to leave the country from new export dock expansion (e.g., Enterprise and Morgan's Point expansion, also its new Neches River facility). If cargoes cannot be diverted out of China to other locations, we're going to see volumes back up into domestic storage. Luckily, this is a good time of year to get that volume put into working storage, but if the situation lingers for too long, this could cause issues.

Takeaways

Many pundits had very valid concerns about what would happen to NGL prices before additional export capacity came online, and before this tariff situation, we looked to have dodged that problem. With the issue now fresh again on our minds, investors need to watch what midstream firms with exposure say in upcoming presentations / Q1 conference calls on what exposure they have, how they are protecting their business, and how their 2025 outlooks are changing.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Tariff Whipsaw: Mixed Signals And Market Shocks
data.description:
By Seema Shah, Chief Global Strategist

Markets remain in flux following President Trump’s announcement of a 90-day pause on most reciprocal tariffs — excluding China, where import duties were raised sharply. Initially reported at 125%, the White House clarified Thursday that the effective rate on Chinese goods is now 145%. That unexpected revision triggered a sharp reversal in investor sentiment: after Wednesday’s historic rally, U.S. equities fell steeply on Thursday, with the S&P 500 down 3.5%, the Nasdaq dropping 4.3%, and the Dow shedding over 1,000 points.

The initial market surge reflected hopes that the administration was prioritizing financial stability, particularly as bond yields reflected signs of stress. But Thursday’s clarification revealed that policy uncertainty remains elevated. Volatility spiked again, and markets quickly recalibrated as concerns about inflation, policy credibility, and the global growth outlook reemerged.

Even with March’s softer-than-expected CPI data, the economic outlook remains fragile. The U.S. average effective tariff rate is still the highest in the post-World War II era. China's retaliatory actions, including new restrictions on U.S. services imports, add to the risk that global trade slows meaningfully in the months ahead.

For investors, the takeaway is nuanced: policy remains reactive, not resolved. Staying invested, diversified, and flexible across asset classes is critical. After all, markets typically experience several large pullbacks each year. In an environment where political developments are moving faster than fundamentals, resilience matters more than ever.

Original Post

data.title: March CPI Report: Risks Delayed, Not Removed
data.description:
By Christian Floro, CFA, CMT, Market Strategist | Seema Shah, Chief Global Strategist

Report details

  • Monthly headline inflation declined 0.1% in March, lower than expected and the smallest monthly variation since May 2020, bringing the annual rate to 2.4% - from 2.8% prior. Core inflation, which strips out food and energy, also came in lower than expected, increasing 0.1% in March as the annual rate decelerated to 2.8% - from 3.1% prior. Although headline prices seeing deflation for the first time since the pandemic and core price increases continuing to moderate is encouraging, the release is now outdated given the fluid policy environment on trade.
  • Food prices increased 0.4% in March, with prices for food at home rising 0.5% as four of the six major grocery store food groups increased. The closely watched eggs category saw a more moderate 5.9% increase in the month (from 10.4% prior), with price increases likely to continue to soften as wholesale prices have collapsed. Energy prices decreased 2.4% in March, weighing on overall inflation and helped by a large 6.3% drop in gasoline prices.
  • The modest increase in core inflation has remained predominantly a function of services prices, which increased 0.1%. Shelter remained the largest contributor to this category, with owners' equivalent rent rising 0.4%, the largest increase since October 2024. Meanwhile, weighing on the overall increase in services has been a continued softening in demand for travel, likely driven by uncertainty triggered by trade policy. Lodging away from home prices dropped 3.5% while airline fares fell 5.3%, both the largest since the fall of 2021.
  • Core goods prices returned to deflation in the month, decreasing 0.1%, driven by a 1.1% decline in medical care commodities and used vehicle prices. This more than offset the 0.4% increase in apparel, which is predominantly imported from China, which may be showing early signs of tariff pass through, albeit the overall effect remains muted at this stage. Looking ahead, the impact of rising tariffs will likely continue to pressure core goods prices higher in the months ahead.
  • The Fed's preferred supercore inflation measure, which excludes shelter from core services and is primarily driven by wage costs, declined by 0.2%, leading the annual rate to decline from 3.8% to 2.9%, the lowest since March 2021.

Policy outlook

Thursday's inflation report, which was softer than expected, should provide some reassurance to markets, which were rocked in the past week with trade policy-related volatility. Fed speakers have been reiterating their inflation caution in recent days, but if inflation pressures prove to be more sanguine, then they will be more willing to provide the necessary support to the economy.

The reprieve is likely to be temporary, however, as tariffs will trigger a surge in inflation. The increase in U.S. tariffs on China's imports will deliver meaningful upward pressure to costs unless supply chains are diverted to other economies, so inflation risks remain elevated, even after yesterday's 90-day reprieve to the rest of the world. The Fed ultimately has no room for complacency, so rate cuts may be fairly constrained. We anticipate three to four cuts this year, with a severe labor market slowdown or simmering systemic risks the only factors to drive a more aggressive response.

Original Post

data.title: Coinbase: Crypto Reset Near Complete (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Along with the market slump, Coinbase Global, Inc. (NASDAQ:COIN) has fallen back to yearly lows. Unfortunately, cryptocurrencies, like Bitcoin, weren't similarly wiped out providing a far clearer path upwards. My investment thesis is more Bullish on the stock after the froth is gone with a potential double bottom around $160, but the company likely comes out with weak Q2'25 targets.

Resetting Revenue Targets

At the recent highs, Coinbase was a tough buy due to the stretched valuation combined with elevated crypto valuations. In essence, the stock was priced for perfection and the company faced the recent scenario where the underlying crypto assets slipped, leading to a reduction in expected revenues.

Though, so far, Bitcoin hasn't fallen dramatically, only slipping to $80K. The cryptocurrency is still above the $60K levels from last year, while falling from recent highs around $100K.

With the hype burned out of the crypto market, Coinbase can build on a more normal transaction base starting in Q2. After all, the big revenue ramp in 2024 only got the business back to the 2022 peak levels of $2.3+ billion in quarterly revenues when Bitcoin originally reached $50K.

The main cryptocurrency doubled and Coinbase didn't even reach the prior peak revenue levels. The company has even made progress to diversify away from Bitcoin trading revenues, with Subscription and Services revenues slowly growing to $641 million in Q4.

With Bitcoin surging from $60K at the start of October in Q4 to $100K by mid-December, the business was in an ideal transaction environment during Q4'24. Coinbase saw transaction revenues soar 88% QoQ to $1.35 billion in a jump that likely isn't repeatable for the rest of 2025.

Coinbase would appear to face some lower transaction revenues during Q1'25, leading to a possible collapse in transactions during Q2. The company only saw Q2/Q3 transaction revenues around $650 million last year, some $900 million lower than the spike in Q4.

As back in 2022 and 2023, Coinbase saw both the assets under management and transaction volume slump when crypto prices fell. As an example, transaction values slipped from $1,650 billion in 2021 to only $468 billion by 2023.

Coinbase produced $2.2 billion in Q4 revenues and the current estimates are for quarterly revenue only dipping to ~$1.95 billion over the next few quarters. Back in 2022, the business plunged more than 50% to a low of only $0.6 billion.

When reporting Q4 results, Coinbase discussed Q1'25 transaction revenue of $750 million through February 11. The company was on pace for another quarter of $1.5 billion in transaction revenues, but Bitcoin fell from $95K to below $80K to start April.

The analyst estimates appear far too high for the reality of the crypto market, where transaction revenues are volatile. Revenues tend to spike like in Q1'24 and Q4'24 followed by much weaker quarters where both transaction volumes and values plunge.

Investors have to expect a weak outlook for Q2 after this volatile tariff policy likely leads to people pulling back on trading.

More Stock Appeal

The stock is far more appealing here, with the market cap back down to $42 billion. Coinbase ran to the prior peak of nearly $350 and the stock is suddenly down over 50% on the anticipation of weaker revenues.

Coinbase remains highly profitable with the consensus estimates predicting a $7+ EPS for the next few years, leading to a P/E multiple of ~23x. The risk is that these numbers need to be cut, but ultimately the stock is at an attractive valuation based on the crypto market growing over time to justify a premium valuation.

The crypto platform only has transaction expenses at 14% of net revenue, making the business highly profitable. Coinbase is spending about $550 million in adjusted operating expenses on a quarterly basis, to the point where Subscription and Services revenues targeted at $725 million for Q1'25 nearly cover most operating expenses.

The company has a massive cash balance of $9.3 billion due to generating net income of $2.6 billion last year. The crypto business is printing money regardless of the volatility.

Coinbase just needs to adjust revenue expectations for the reset to be complete. The company will report Q1 results and reset expectations for Q2 in a few weeks, providing an opportunity to use the double bottom here around $160 as an initial entry point with some capital held back for even lower prices and a cut in revenue expectations. A standard method is to invest up to 50% of a full position now, with 50% held back for potentially lower prices.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Coinbase Global is far more interesting here, with the stock trading at the yearly lows and some of the excess in cryptocurrencies ringed from the market. Investors should use the weakness to start building a position, knowing some potential downside exists on a lowering of revenue expectations for the next quarter.

data.title: U.S. Rows Back On Reciprocal Tariffs: Temporary Respite Or Radical Reset?
data.description:
While the acute near-term risk of a recessionary feedback loop has been averted, the chronic problem of policy volatility and related uncertainties persists.

What happened

On April 9, US President Donald Trump announced the following:

  • An immediate 90-day pause of the additional “reciprocal tariff” rates applied to imports from a wide range of trading partners that were announced on April 2.
  • An initial 10% baseline tariff on US imports from all countries (with ongoing exemptions for certain products and minerals).
  • An immediate increase in the tariff on imports from China to 125% from 104%.

The president said that the pause on the additional reciprocal tariffs (with the exception of China) was due to the willingness of those countries to “negotiate a solution to... trade barriers, tariffs, currency manipulation and non-monetary tariffs” and because they had not “retaliated... against the US” following the prior announcements on April 2.

Automobiles and automotive parts imports into the US remain subject to a separate 25% tariff, as do imports of steel and aluminum. Imports from Mexico and Canada that do not adhere to the terms of 2020’s trade deal also remain subject to a 25% tariff.

What does this mean?

A positive interpretation of the announcement on April 9 would center on the following: It demonstrates a willingness to change course in the face of increasing evidence of negative economic and financial fallout. The so-called “guard rails” — that is, a negative response from financial markets will constrain how far the president and the administration are willing to go —exist.

The pain threshold of the president and the US administration appears to be lower than financial markets had feared following the announcement of the reciprocal tariffs on April 2 and the market turbulence that followed. The pause on the additional reciprocal tariffs will now lead to an acceleration of trading partners’ efforts to negotiate deals with the US.

A less positive interpretation of recent developments would include: The huge uncertainty surrounding the US policy outlook and its economic and financial market implications, particularly but not only related to trade, will persist. This will continue to have a negative impact on consumer and corporate sentiment, potentially reducing households’ propensity to consume and businesses’ propensity to invest.

Trade tensions between the world’s two biggest economies, the US and China, accounting for around 40% of global GDP combined, have continued to escalate. The additional reciprocal tariffs on trading partners other than China have merely been paused. They could be reintroduced, or increased even, at any point. A baseline 10% tariff on US imports still applies, as do 25% tariffs on key products and non-United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement (USMCA)-compliant imports from Mexico and Canada.

All things considered, a medical analogy arguably best sums up the current situation. The acute near-term risk of a negative feedback loop of extreme financial market turbulence and recessionary economic conditions looks to have been averted, at least for the time being. However, the chronic problem of exceptionally high US policy volatility, related uncertainties and negative spillovers onto economic prospects is still very much with us.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Deep Dive Into Amazon's 2024 Shareholder Letter
data.description:
Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) just released its Shareholder Letter. The main investment thesis remains: expand markets and then work on expanding margins. The results have been very positive, as we can see in the next section, but it is more important to understand how this will work for the future now that the company is already a behemoth.

Financial Results

Amazon posted solid numbers this year. Total revenue climbed from $575 billion to $638 billion—that’s an 11% increase year-over-year.

Here’s the breakdown by segment:

North America: Up 10%, rising from $353B to $387B

International: Grew 9%, from $131B to $143B

AWS: The standout performer—jumped 19%, from $91B to $108B

Operating income nearly doubled—going from $36.9B (with a 6.4% margin) to $68.6B (now a 10.8% margin). That’s an 86% jump, which is pretty striking and points to major efficiency gains across the board. Free cash flow, adjusted for equipment leases, ticked up slightly from $35.5B to $36.2B.

Bottom line: All the major business units—North America, International, and especially AWS—showed healthy growth. AWS is still on a tear, thanks to ongoing demand for cloud and AI tools.

Operations and Customer Side

Amazon kept expanding its selection and kept prices low—according to an independent study, it remained the lowest-priced online retailer in the U.S. for the eighth year in a row. On top of that, Prime members saw the fastest delivery speeds ever—again—for the second year straight.

There were some big tech moves here. Amazon rolled out new AI-focused infrastructure, including its latest chip, Trainium2. It also launched Amazon Nova, a new family of high-end foundation models, while continuing to build out SageMaker and Bedrock for generative AI.

Lots of fresh content dropped this year—new seasons of Fallout, Reacher, The Boys, and Rings of Power, plus movies like Road House, The Idea of You, and Red One. On the sports side, Thursday Night Football continued, the UEFA Champions League came to Europe, and deals are already in place to bring NBA and NASCAR content in 2025. Also, Apple TV+ was added to Prime Video Channels.

All of these moves show Amazon broadening its product and service offerings, while staying fast and affordable.

Amazon’s Ethos: A Culture Built on Asking “Why?”

CEO Andy Jassy talks a lot about what he calls a “Why Culture”—basically, a mindset where people are encouraged to constantly ask, “Why not?” or “Why can’t we do this better?” That kind of thinking has powered a lot of Amazon’s biggest shifts over the years.

A few past “Why” moments that changed the company: Expanding beyond books into every kind of product, letting third-party sellers list on the same product pages as Amazon, and launching AWS so developers wouldn’t need their own data centers

Now, what will matter are the “next-generation Whys.” These “next-generation Whys” are the driving questions shaping Amazon’s strategy in 2024 and beyond.

One of the biggest ones: Why is AI such a big deal? According to Andy Jassy, AI is on track to transform almost every customer experience. While early uses of AI have focused on streamlining internal tasks and cutting costs—things like automating support or internal processes—the real game-changers are coming.

The Why question will keep fueling growth pursuits

Amazon’s leadership is clear about what’s going to fuel its next phase of growth. Looking ahead, the company is placing big bets across a few major areas—each one tied to long-term trends and backed by heavy investment.

At the top of that list is artificial intelligence. AWS is going all-in here, building out its AI offerings with tools like Trainium2 (Amazon’s latest custom chip), as well as platforms like SageMaker, Bedrock, and Amazon Nova. The goal is to make AI more cost-effective and easier to use, both for big enterprises and everyday consumers. Demand for generative AI, in particular, is expected to explode. Amazon sees itself playing a role in everything from software development and robotics to healthcare and education, and it’s positioning AWS to be the backbone for that transformation.

The play will be to adopt market expansion strategies like the ones the company deployed in the past, drive consumer costs down to expand the addressable market, and then focus on expanding margins. This will be valid for personal assistants, retail delivery, and healthcare. First, the company builds infrastructure that drives convenience up and costs down, and only after it recognizes a solid offering does it go back to profitability – this has been their playbook since inception.

Driving margin expansion

One of the biggest levers Amazon has to improve its margins—both now and over the long haul—is scale. Nowhere is that more evident than in AWS. As more companies turn to AWS for their AI and machine learning workloads, Amazon can spread the massive infrastructure costs—think data centers, networking, and compute—across a broader base of customers. That higher utilization helps drive down the unit cost of running these services, especially as demand for AI ramps up. On top of that, AWS has been designing its own custom chips like Trainium2, which significantly reduce core compute costs. By relying less on third-party hardware and more on its own silicon, Amazon can lower expenses internally while still pricing competitively.

In its retail business, Amazon’s been steadily fine-tuning operations to reduce overhead and improve fulfillment economics. The company has restructured its logistics network to focus more on “regionalization”—basically, keeping inventory closer to where people live. This setup cuts down one of Amazon’s biggest cost drivers: transportation. Less distance means lower shipping costs, faster delivery, and better inventory accuracy. That all translates to less waste and better margins in the fulfillment network. Internally, Amazon is also tightening up its operations by trimming bureaucracy—reducing unnecessary layers of management and approval processes.

AI is also a margin story—not just a growth story. While training AI models gets most of the attention, Amazon knows that inference (the process of actually using trained models) will eventually dominate in terms of total usage. That’s where the real opportunity lies for improving the margin. Through techniques like model distillation, caching, and ongoing hardware improvements, Amazon can lower the cost of inference—helping AWS increase gross margins on its AI services over time. And with more high-value generative AI tools and agent-based services coming online, there’s potential for premium pricing in certain enterprise use cases, especially when those services are delivered at scale with operational efficiency.

Culturally, Amazon is sticking to its “operate like a startup” philosophy—running lean teams and prioritizing builders over layers of management. By keeping the ratio of individual contributors high relative to managers, Amazon channels more resources into making and shipping products, rather than managing internal complexity. This approach helps keep costs in check while supporting fast-paced innovation.

Beyond its core businesses, Amazon continues to build out high-margin adjacencies. Advertising, for example, is a quiet powerhouse—placing targeted ads within the retail environment is one of the company’s most profitable revenue streams. Healthcare is another area with potential. If Amazon can simplify how people get prescriptions through Amazon Pharmacy or streamline primary care via One Medical, those services could operate at significantly better margins than traditional e-commerce. Over time, they could become meaningful contributors to overall profitability.

And finally, there’s the big-picture view. Amazon is still taking big swings—like Project Kuiper and the expansion of AI infrastructure—but these aren’t bets without a long-term plan. The company expects these capital-intensive projects to generate strong returns at scale. As those services mature and attract more users, the up-front investment gets amortized across a larger base. That’s when the free cash flow starts to accelerate—and operating income margins start to follow.

Dissecting Amazon’s Valuation

When it comes to valuing Amazon, the usual retail comparisons don’t really cut it. Most analysts tend to group it alongside the tech heavyweights—names like Apple, Microsoft, Alphabet, and Meta—rather than traditional retailers. And it makes sense why. Amazon isn’t just a place to buy stuff online; it’s built a massive ecosystem that blends hardware, cloud infrastructure, software, digital services, and advertising. Because of that, metrics like price-to-earnings and price-to-sales are often lined up against what we see in the “FAAMG” group. These are companies with global reach, deep product portfolios, and the kind of balance sheets that let them invest at scale, even during downturns

As for Amazon’s current numbers, they don’t fit neatly into one box. Its P/E ratio, hovering around 32.8, lands in a sort of middle ground. It’s higher than Alphabet (about 19) or Meta (23), which are more mature in the ad business, but still below growth-centric names like Netflix (46.5) and even Walmart (37.6). That suggests the market sees Amazon as more than just a retailer—but maybe not as a pure tech company either. Its valuation seems to price in real upside in areas like AWS and advertising, but with some caution.

The P/S ratio tells a similar story. Amazon sits around 3.0, which is significantly above Walmart’s 1.1, but still well below companies like Meta (8.7), Alphabet (5.6), or Netflix (10.4). This reflects a structural truth: retail margins are just thinner. Even with Amazon’s growing high-margin segments, its overall profitability is still anchored by a vast, capital-heavy retail operation. That pulls down the multiple compared to more streamlined, software-based businesses.

Still, the fact that Amazon trades so far above Walmart shows how the market views it: not just as a store, but as a hybrid platform with meaningful contributions from high-margin segments like AWS, ads, and subscriptions. Investors aren’t just betting on where Amazon is today—they’re pricing in where it could go next.

So what drives Amazon’s valuation today? A big part of it is its multi-segment business model. On one hand, you have the core e-commerce platform, which moves a ton of volume but doesn’t generate huge margins. Amazon deliberately reinvests in fulfillment, price competitiveness, and logistics, often at the cost of short-term profitability. But on the other hand, you’ve got AWS, a high-margin cloud business that regularly delivers the majority of Amazon’s operating income. That segment alone warrants a higher valuation multiple, similar to Microsoft Azure or Google Cloud. Then there’s advertising—sponsored product placements and display ads are growing quickly and carry excellent margins. Together, AWS and advertising help justify a valuation more in line with tech than retail.

Zooming out, Amazon’s valuation sits in a space that reflects its hybrid identity. Its 3.0x P/S ratio is far above that of a traditional retailer like Walmart, but lower than most of its tech peers—mirroring its blended model. Its P/E around 33 is also somewhere between growth-heavy optimism and mature-business pragmatism.

Bear Case for Amazon

While Amazon has a lot going for it, there are also meaningful risks and headwinds that investors need to keep in mind—especially when a company of this size is still betting big on long-term growth. One major concern is overinvestment and the potential for delayed payoffs. Amazon is spending heavily on AI infrastructure—including data centers and its custom Trainium chips—as well as large-scale initiatives like Project Kuiper (satellite internet), robotics, and healthcare. These aren’t cheap ventures.

There’s also execution risk. Amazon has a history of big swings, and not every one of them turned into an AWS-style success story. Projects like Kuiper, Alexa+, or its forays into healthcare could fail to gain traction or deliver the kind of returns the company is banking on. If those bets flop, they could drag on earnings and sentiment for a long time.

In the cloud business, Amazon is still a dominant player, but the competitive landscape is fierce. Microsoft Azure and Google Cloud continue to chip away at AWS’s lead, and a full-blown pricing war could compress margins, especially for AI workloads that are resource-intensive. Even on the hardware front, Amazon is pushing its custom AI chips (like Trainium2), but it’s still up against major players like Nvidia and AMD.

There’s also a broader risk around the AI boom itself—namely, whether it plays to Amazon’s strengths. The company is focused on being a toolmaker with platforms like SageMaker and Bedrock, but companies like Microsoft (via OpenAI) and Google are shaping up as the go-to platforms for enterprise-level AI.

There’s also the human side of the equation. Maintaining Amazon’s “Why Culture” at its current scale is no easy task. As the company grows more complex, there’s always the risk that bureaucracy creeps in and innovation slows. Meanwhile, competition for top-tier AI and cloud talent is intense.

Bottom-line

Amazon continues to straddle two worlds—its low-margin retail foundation, and it's increasingly important, high-margin tech businesses. That mix puts its current valuation somewhere in the middle of the pack. With a P/E ratio of around 32.8 and a price-to-sales ratio near 3.0, Amazon trades above traditional retailers like Walmart, but below pure tech and streaming companies such as Meta, Alphabet and Netflix.

Looking forward, the stock’s potential really hinges on execution. In a bullish scenario, if Amazon successfully leans into AI, continues to grow AWS, and further scales its advertising business, some analysts forecast earnings per share of $10.60 by 2027. Apply a 30x multiple to that, and you get a price target of $318, which would translate to roughly 20.7% annualized returns from a current price of around $181. On the flip side, in a bearish case where growth slows or heavy investments in areas like healthcare or satellite broadband fail to deliver, EPS could land closer to $7.74. At a more conservative 20x multiple, that points to a $154.80 price target, implying a modest –5.1% annualized loss over the same period.

Ultimately, Amazon is still playing from its long-established playbook: expand aggressively, then optimize for margins. That strategy has worked before—most notably with AWS and the third-party Marketplace. As the company pushes into big, capital-intensive areas like AI infrastructure, healthcare, and satellite internet, the payoff may take time.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: AOA: Be Strategic, Consider Trimming
data.description:
The S&P 500 (SPY) has been down 19% from its peak as of the writing of this paragraph, and long-term bonds (BLV) are now 5% below recent highs – see below. It is nearly impossible not to hear panicked investors wonder if now is either a good time to de-risk their portfolios or, instead, ride the storm in hopes of a long-term gain off the current lows.

Within the context of a blended portfolio of stocks and bonds, I will explain below why I think that now is a good time to play defense. For this discussion, the case study is the iShares Core 80/20 Aggressive Allocation ETF (NYSEARCA:AOA).

About iShares Core 80/20 ETF

Simply put, AOA allocates 80% of its assets to stocks and 20% to bonds. More specifically, the 80% and 20% investments are made as follows:

  • 44% in iShares Core S&P ETF TRUST (IVV), a fund that simply tracks the S&P 500
  • 22% in iShares Core MSCI International Developed ETF (IDEV), a non-US stock fund
  • 8% in iShares Core MSCI Emerging Markets (IEMG)
  • Nearly 4% in mid- and small-cap US stocks
  • 19% in a diversified basket of bonds (IUSB) that is roughly 60% government or agency and 40% corporate, with average maturity of 8 years
  • About 3% in others

Due to the heavy allocation to stocks, this can be considered an ideal ETF for investors looking for long-term growth without losing sight of a bit of diversification (not too much) into a mixed bag of fixed-income instruments.

AOA has an average volume traded of about $8 million per day and a bid-ask spread of about 12 cents of a share price of $69 (i.e., roughly 0.2%). This suggests a fairly liquid instrument for most investors, although probably not so much for the higher-frequency trader. The annual management fee of 15 bps is not too bad for a full-package ETF that does quite a bit of diversification for its investors within the confines of the fund’s mandate.

Consider trimming AOA

At a high level, there are a few reasons why the slightly more conservative investor might want to sell some (or all, for now) of their AOA position. The first that comes to mind is the potential false sense of security that being diversified across stocks and bonds may provide.

First, allocating 80% to stocks effectively exposes a portfolio to roughly 90% of the risks associated with being invested in stocks. For example, the annualized volatility and maximum drawdowns in AOA (using month-end price points) have been 14% and 23%, respectively, going back to 2017. A diversified equities portfolio, during the same period, would have been only slightly riskier: 16% volatility and 25% maximum drawdown. It is not much of a surprise that AOA has already dipped about as much from the February peak as international stocks have.

The second reason is much more nuanced and aligned with my (somewhat contrarian) take on investment strategies. In my view, based on my research and multiple backtests, it is much more productive from a risk-adjusted perspective to stay more aggressively invested when the markets are in a “healthier” state. Once price levels drop and volatility rises beyond a minor, business-as-usual blip, it is best to de-risk and wait for the all-clear sign to jump back in.

Consider a portfolio that is invested 80% in SPY and 20% in intermediate-term treasuries – somewhat of a proxy for AOA. Rather than buying and holding such a portfolio, I propose that a better approach would be to do the following:

  • Hold it at 100% exposure only if the underlying 80/20 securities are trading above the 200-day moving average. As the 80/20 strategy rises over time, as one would expect it to, the investor rides the upside over the years.
  • If below the 200-day moving average, sell four-fifths of the position in order to substantially de-risk the portfolio. Doing so helps to prevent the investor from riding the downside to what could eventually be a painful, possibly 40%-plus selloff.
  • In addition, track the trailing 14-day annualized volatility of the underlying 80/20 portfolio. If it is above 20% (roughly the 90th percentile), sell regardless of the 200-day moving average. This helps an investor to de-risk early enough, in case of big trouble brewing.

Since the early 1990s, a standard 80/20 buy-and-hold approach would have produced 9% annualized return on 14% volatility, suggesting Sharpe of 0.5, if I assume a zero risk-free rate for simplicity. The maximum drawdown would have been 45% (using daily prices), and the worst day of trading would have been -9%.

Stepping away from trouble, using the approach described above, would have produced slightly lower absolute returns of 8%. However, volatility would have been only 9% for a much better Sharpe of 0.66. The maximum drawdown would have been only 23%, and the worst trading day, -5%. See chart below and notice how the crises of 2000-2003, 2008, 2020, and 2022 would have been only minor headaches for the loss mitigation approach.

In conclusion

Timing entries and exists into stocks and bonds using one’s convictions can work at times, but not always – I would say 50% to 55% of the time, at best. But using price action and volatility as a way to identify potential pitfalls, then stepping out of the way to avoid being caught in a downward spiral, can substantially improve risk-adjusted performance.

Today, both volatility and price action suggest that it is a good time to be cautious with a stock-and-bond strategy. For this reason, I would de-risk my portfolio until the market shows enough signs of being in a constructive mode once again.

data.title: AVMV ETF: Worth A Look For Mid-Cap Value
data.description:
What is AVMV?

The Avantis U.S. Mid Cap Value ETF (NYSEARCA:AVMV) is an actively managed exchange-traded fund (also known as ETF) that invests in mid-cap American listed stocks. The point of this ETF is to have a cheap, passively managed option to track outperforming stocks within the Russell Mid-Cap Index, with the fund manager rebalancing and reviewing the fund daily for investment opportunity.

AVMV's benchmark target is to outperform the Russell Mid-Cap Index, and this article will tell you whether it's worth investing with AVMV or another asset manager. The benchmark index generally includes U.S. mid-capitalization companies across a variety of industries, with higher weights in financials and industrials and usually lower weights in high flying tech stocks. Passive investing ETF's mean that the fund managers don't make many trades, and only rebalance every once in a while, versus active managers, who have the mandate to buy and sell stocks more often if they feel that there's a compelling investment opportunity. I've worked in capital markets credit risk in the fund business at one of the largest banks in Canada, and have found that more often than not, these actively managed ETFs are simply not worth the high fees.

After further review, my analysis shows that AVMV is potentially worth an investment vs. other peers. The fund has beaten its benchmark, while also charging lower fees than its competitors. However, with the recent market sell-off, the fund has seen declines that have wiped its share gains and have seen the fund fall to the same performance since inception as iShares Russell Mid-Cap Value ETF (IWS), the passive Russell Mid-Cap Index tracking ETF. That said, the 2024 returns were strong and I think it's worth keeping an eye on this fund as a potential bounceback if Trump's tariffs are revised.

Fund Breakdown

AVMV was launched on November 7, 2023, by Avantis (a part of American Century Investments) to provide investment results that outperform to the Russell Mid-Cap Index. That said, the fund attempts to find value within the broader scope of the index, noting in its prospectus that "The fund seeks to achieve higher expected returns by selecting securities of companies with higher profitability and value characteristics, as well as smaller market capitalizations relative to others within the fund’s mid cap investment universe". As per the fund documents, value is segregated based on book/price ratios, while profitability is based on adjusted cash flow to book value ratio. All that to say, the fund is not using too many criteria to narrow down their investments, but as of late 2024, it seemed to be doing the trick.

The fund has a NAV of ~$100MM and a daily volume of over 28,000 shares traded. The idea of the fund makes sense - though the portfolio turnover is around 10%, so the managers are actually treating this like more of a passive fund. That makes sense given the fees are low, which I'll get to in a second. But overall, it seems like the fund has a couple simple value/profitability screens, and selects funds without too many changes - and it was working before Trump destabilized the market. Here are the returns since inception vs. IWS, the purely passive Russell MidCap Index tracking alternative.

The fund also employs just a 0.20% yearly ETF expense ratio for fees to the asset manager (as of Dec. 31/24), which is actually a bit lower than IWS, which is 0.23%. There is an option, per the prospectus, to have the fee rise to 0.25% max. But ultimately, the fund is decent, cheap, actively managed and has done a decent job over the past year.

Previous Results & Fund Portfolio

When comparing AVMV's portfolio to peers, there are some notable differences in the portfolio breakdown. As of Dec. 31, 2024, the portfolio was quite different than the index benchmark, with additional sector focus on consumer discretionary, financials and energy. The fund has considerably less weight in technology and utilities.

From a risk perspective, the fund's beta (at 1.27) is higher than the benchmark, which makes sense given the higher weight in volatile energy stocks. That said, the weighted book/market and profit/book values are aligned with the prospectus language in that they are screening for value and higher profitability. Unfortunately, AVMV's Sharpe ratio for the fund is -0.18, below the score of 1+ to prove strong risk-adjusted returns, as mid-caps have been hammered recently. And from a macroeconomic perspective, mid-cap growth stocks are more sensitive to interest rate fluctuations. With the Federal Reserve taking a wait and see approach to rate cuts, mid-cap stocks may be left in limbo, after the last few years of underperformance due to higher borrowing costs.

Risks & Bottom Line

The bottom line for this fund, in my opinion, is that investors should take a wait and see approach before buying this fund versus a passive benchmark. I appreciate that an active fund has a 0.20% expense ratio and has an easy-to-understand investment criteria, along with decent liquidity. But AVMV has come crashing down to earth (and IWS) post the Trump tariff collapse and I don't see a resolution anytime soon. Keep an eye on AVMV if you're looking to get mid-cap value exposure, as it may be an outperformer compared to the passive benchmark.

data.title: Why I'm Downgrading Broadcom To A Hold (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Intro

After my buy rating in early January, Broadcom Inc. (NASDAQ:AVGO) has dropped over 30% with Trump's tariff headwinds being a key cause. In the below analysis, I determine that most technical signs suggest further weakness in the stock. While there are some near term positive signals in the indicators and weakening bearish momentum in the Ichimoku Clouds, they likely signal a reprieve rather than a true bullish reversal. For the fundamentals, most recent earnings were respectable and guidance was satisfactory as growth is expected to remain robust despite some slowdowns. The P/S ratio has contracted to reflect this but remains above average, suggesting slight overvaluation. However, with the company announcing a new share repurchase program, the fundamental setup is likely net positive at this point. Overall, I believe Broadcom has become a hold since while the long term fundamentals may be net attractive, the technicals don't show a bottom yet.

Technical Analysis

The Basics

Trends, Support & Resistance

Broadcom remains in a near term downtrend that has been pressuring the stock since mid-February. The trend line last acted as resistance in late March and given its sharp trajectory, could be worrying about the bulls. While the trend line is quickly becoming the most relevant source of resistance there are three more key levels that I want to discuss. The first is in the mid-160s which represents the recent downside gap in the stock. This area seems to have acted as a bit of resistance on the previous trading day. The next key level is in the mid-180s. This area was a ceiling for the stock from early October to mid-December while being a floor for much of March. Lastly, the round number 200 could be a significant level as it was key support in late January while being resistance in early March.

Moving Averages & Indicators

The 50 day SMA has remained above its 200-day counterpart for the duration of the past six months, showing that long term bullishness has generally been sustained. However, since late March, the deviation between the SMAs has been decreasing and so momentum has been on the decline. Late last month, the stock also broke beneath the 200-day SMA as an important sign of weakness. As for the EMAs, the 9 day EMA had a bearish crossover with the 21 day EMA in late February to show that the near term had turned bearish for Broadcom. Currently, the gap between the EMAs is significant and shows that near term bearish momentum is still very strong.

The stock was able to break back above the Bollinger Bands midline for a brief period in February, but ultimately, it slid below again. This was an early indication that a downtrend may have been developing. Since crossing back below in late February, the stock has not challenged the midline, and so bearishness has been considerable. Days ago, the stock was below the lower band and so the recent bounce is not surprising as it was oversold. For the MACD, the indicator was unable to break back above the signal line in late March as a sign of persistent bears. Since then, the gap between the lines generally expanded to show a worsening in sentiment until recently when the histogram turned bullish for Broadcom. The MACD did make a new six month low recently, however, confirming the declines in the stock. For the RSI, it currently has a reading of around 36 as it has bounced back from a sub-30 level. The bears are therefore still in control by a wide margin, despite ceding some ground to the bulls. Like the MACD, the RSI confirms the recent plunge in the stock with its own six-month low. Lastly, for the stochastics, there was a bullish crossover that just occurred. The %K's trajectory has turned significantly positive, and the gap between the lines seems to be expanding in a healthy manner. The %K has also exited the 20 zone as another sign of weakening bears.

Takeaway

The technical outlook remains bearish overall for Broadcom. The chart shows that the stock remains in a near term downtrend with resistance above, while the moving averages show weakness in both the near and long terms. As for the indicators, most signals were bearish but there are some positive signs that are beginning to develop. The MACD histogram has turned bullish for the past two days and the stochastics just had a bullish crossover. Therefore, hope is not completely lost for the bulls as the stock may be seeing some reprieve from the intense selling pressure.

Fibonacci

Retracement

Using the late January peak and the six-month low set just days ago, potential retracement resistance zones are identified above. The nearest level is 23.6% in the mid to high 160s. This area may have acted as a bit of resistance on the previous trading day and may continue, preventing further gains in the stock. If Broadcom is able to breakout from this level, the next challenge would be the 38.2% level, which is just above the round number 180.

Projection

Using the late January high again and the early March low with the retracement to around 200, the Fibonacci Projection shows potential support levels for the stock. Two trading sessions ago, Broadcom broke beneath the 61.8% level that was in the mid-150s, but it was able to recover. On the previous trading day, the stock seems to have found some support at this 61.8% level, as the candlestick was able to close above it. Do note that the next support level indicated here at the 100% level is below 130 and so other than the 61.8% level, support from the Fibonacci Projection is relatively far away.

Ichimoku Cloud (Advanced)

The stock's recent plunge has seen it fall far beneath the cloud. This shows there is general bearishness currently in Broadcom. However, the cloud itself remains positive for the time being as the Leading Span A is still above Leading Span B until late April. Moving into May, the cloud may be a key source of resistance as the thickness continues to increase with the Leading Span A continuing to fall. In terms of the near term, the Conversion Line broke beneath the Base Line back in late February to show that momentum had shifted bearish. The gap between these two lines is significant currently, but with the Base Line's trajectory worse than the Conversion Line's, bearish momentum may actually be slowing in the near term. Lastly, the Lagging Span has, of course, rebounded along with the stock, but it's still far from late February levels in Broadcom. If the stock were to hold relatively steady from here, the gap between the Lagging Span and Broadcom's historic price would decrease into early March to show a decrease in bearish momentum.

Fundamental Analysis

Earnings

Quarterly Results

Broadcom reported their FY2025 Q1 earnings in early March and showed respectable results overall. The company posted revenues of $14.9 billion, up 25% YoY while adjusted EPS was $1.60, up 45% from the prior year period's $1.10. Their Infrastructure Software segment saw superior growth with revenues increasing 47% while their Semiconductor Solutions segment only saw growth of 11%. As a result, the contribution to revenues of their semiconductor business has shrunk to 55% versus the prior 62%. However, they reported that their AI semiconductor solutions saw 77% growth to total $4.1 billion within this segment, and so clearly the AI boom is continuing for the company. Both revenues and EPS were able to surpass expectations as they beat by $325.27 million and $0.09 respectively, showing outperformance relative to analyst estimates. As shown in the above chart, revenue growth has slowed from the prior quarter and is now back to only around average levels relative to the past few years. For cash flow from operations, they reported $6.1 billion, up 27% over the year ago quarter. As shown above, this represents high above-average growth that is only best by FY2022 Q4. Overall, I believe it is clear that Broadcom's fundamentals remained strong through their Q1 as growth was still healthy in key financial metrics.

Guidance

Broadcom provided brief guidance figures to present their outlook for FY2025 Q2. They are expecting to see revenues of approximately $14.9 billion which represents growth of 19% YoY. This continues the trend of growth slowdown from their Q1. Specifically for their AI semiconductors, they are projecting $4.4 billion in revenue for their Q2. With FY2024 Q2 AI revenues being $3.1 billion, this guidance is implying growth of 41.9% YoY. This is also a slowdown for their AI products, as they saw 77% growth in this figure for their Q1. Overall, a slowdown in growth was to be expected as the growth base continues to increase and so as a whole, I would still consider these guidance figure to be robust for the company. In addition, Seeking Alpha currently has a Revisions Grade of A for Broadcom as 97.22% of FY1 Revisions have been upward over the past 90 days. This shows that confidence for the outlook is highly strong at this point.

Valuation

Price To Sales Ratio [P/S]

The P/S ratio currently sits at 14.01 after significant contraction to start 2025. The multiple was significantly above 20 and is now slightly above the average level relative to the past few years. I believe this ratio shows around fair valuation to perhaps slight overvaluation for Broadcom stock. As I discussed earlier, for their Q1, the company saw their revenue growth dip back to around average levels. For their Q2, their guidance shows that growth will slow a bit more to 19% which seems to be a slightly below average level compared to prior years. The P/S ratio has contracted significantly to reflect this but remains a bit above its multiyear average, leading me to believe there is mild overvaluation in Broadcom stock. The current median P/S ratio for the Information Technology sector is only 2.36 but with Broadcom having a strong leadership position in highly-sought-after ASICs, I believe this premium is to be expected for such a dominant company.

Stock Buybacks

As reported by Seeking Alpha on April 7th, Broadcom announced a new share repurchase program that will run through December 31st, 2025. They are planning to buy back up to $10 billion of their own stock through both the open market and private negotiations. In my view, this is a clear message that the company is confident in their fundamentals and believe that now is a great opportunity to buy back their stock at an attractive valuation.

Takeaway

Overall, I believe Broadcom is around fairly valued, as the P/S ratio's contraction quite accurately reflects the results and guidance that the company provided in their latest earnings report. However, the announcement of the share repurchase program may indicate that management is highly confident over the direction of the business and that the stock may actually be cheap relative to growth prospects that are unknown to the public at this point. Therefore, I believe at the current valuation, Broadcom is still relatively attractive as the fundamentals may be stronger than previously thought.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the weak technicals and modestly bullish fundamentals lead me to believe that Broadcom is currently a hold. For the technicals, most indications are negative, and the few positives likely show a near term reprieve rather than a bullish reversal. For the fundamentals, most recent earnings were respectable and guidance was overall satisfactory despite a growth slowdown being projected. The P/S ratio has contracted to reflect this but remains a bit higher than expected, potentially showing some overvaluation. As stated above, the company did just announce a buyback program, however, and so there are reasons to be bullish on the stock at this valuation. Overall, with this mixture of technical and fundamental signals, I believe a hold rating is most appropriate for Broadcom currently.
